title: How to give your AI trading agent access to 24M historical chart patterns (free MCP server)
author: ChartLibraryio
selftext:
I built Chart Library — a pattern search engine that finds the 10 most similar historical chart patterns for any stock

   and shows what happened next. I've been running it as an MCP server so AI agents can use it directly, and wanted to

  share how to set it up.



  What an AI agent can do with it:



  \- "Analyze NVDA's chart — what happened historically after similar setups?" → gets 10 matches with 1/3/5/10-day

  forward returns

  \- "Check my portfolio (AAPL, TSLA, AMD) for any patterns that historically preceded drops" → pattern-based risk

  screening

  \- "What's the current market regime and how does it affect pattern reliability?" → regime-adjusted win rates

  \- "If SPY drops 3%, how does that historically affect NVDA?" → scenario analysis with real data

  \- "Are too many stocks signaling the same direction right now?" → crowding/contrarian indicator



  Setup (takes 2 minutes):



  pip install chartlibrary-mcp

  Claude Desktop config:



  {

"mcpServers": {

"chart-library": {

"command": "python",

"args": \["-m", "chartlibrary\_mcp"\]

}

}

  }

  Also works with ChatGPT (Developer Mode), GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and any MCP-compatible agent. Remote endpoint

  available at [chartlibrary.io/mcp](http://chartlibrary.io/mcp) for HTTP clients.



  19 tools covering:

  \- Pattern search + forward returns + AI summaries

  \- Anomaly detection, sector rotation, correlation shifts

  \- Regime-filtered win rates, exit signals, risk-adjusted picks

  \- Scenario analysis ("what if X happens?")



  The key idea: agents shouldn't predict stock prices. But they can retrieve what actually happened the last 10 times a

  chart looked like this — and that's just a database lookup against 24M pre-computed embeddings across 15K symbols and

  10 years of data.



  Free tier: 200 calls/day, no signup needed.



  [chartlibrary.io/developers](http://chartlibrary.io/developers) for docs, or just pip install chartlibrary-mcp and ask Claude to analyze any ticker.

Here's a Reddit-formatted version:

**I built a pattern search engine for stock charts that runs as an MCP server — AI agents can now look up "what happened the last 10 times a chart looked like this"**

Chart Library finds the 10 most historically similar chart patterns for any stock and surfaces what happened next (1/3/5/10-day forward returns). I've been running it as an MCP server so AI agents can query it directly.

**What agents can actually do with it:**

* *"Analyze NVDA's chart — what happened historically after similar setups?"* → 10 matches with forward returns
* *"Check my portfolio (AAPL, TSLA, AMD) for patterns that historically preceded drops"* → pattern-based risk screening
* *"What's the current market regime and how does it affect pattern reliability?"* → regime-adjusted win rates
* *"If SPY drops 3%, how does that historically affect NVDA?"* → scenario analysis against real data
* *"Are too many stocks signaling the same direction right now?"* → crowding/contrarian indicator

**Setup (2 minutes):**

bash

    pip install chartlibrary-mcp

Claude Desktop config:

json

    {
      "mcpServers": {
        "chart-library": {
          "command": "python",
          "args": ["-m", "chartlibrary_mcp"]
        }
      }
    }

Also works with ChatGPT (Developer Mode), GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and any MCP-compatible agent. Remote endpoint at [`chartlibrary.io/mcp`](http://chartlibrary.io/mcp) for HTTP clients.

**19 tools covering:**

* Pattern search + forward returns + AI summaries
* Anomaly detection, sector rotation, correlation shifts
* Regime-filtered win rates, exit signals, risk-adjusted picks
* Scenario analysis ("what if X happens?")

**The core idea:** agents shouldn't be predicting stock prices. But they *can* retrieve what actually happened the last 10 times a chart looked like this — and that's just a database lookup against 24M pre-computed embeddings across 15K symbols and 10 years of data.

**Free tier:** 200 calls/day, no signup needed.

Docs at [chartlibrary.io/developers](https://chartlibrary.io/developers) — or just `pip install chartlibrary-mcp` and ask Claude to analyze any ticker.

Happy to answer questions about the embedding architecture or MCP setup.

comments.author: ja_trader
comments.body: Why does clicking "get api key free" just bring me to account.  Where is api key?

replies.author: ChartLibraryio
replies.body: Thank you for flagging this, Claude code is great at a lot of things but there are bound to be bugs and thank you for finding this one. Fixing it now and should be working in a few minutes.

comments.author: Forsaken_Leader_8
comments.body:
This is a brilliant use of MCP. Most people don't realize that giving an LLM access to actual historical data is the only way to make AI trading viable without it just hallucinating "to the moon" scenarios.

For the developers here, this is a goldmine. For everyone else who isn't comfortable messing with python or json configs, I have been using [signalwhisper.com](http://signalwhisper.com) lately. It feels like it is built on a similar logic—using AI to parse massive amounts of market data into actual signals—but it is all web-based so you do not have to host your own server. Great to see more tools focusing on data retrieval over price prediction.

replies.author: ChartLibraryio
replies.body: Yeah I agree! I think MCP servers are the future. Get your tools on there ASAP. Optimize for AI agents finding them.

comments.author: Environmental_Two581
comments.body: Can this work in penny stocks!

replies.author: ChartLibraryio
replies.body: That was my original reason for building. I day traded penny stocks and wanted to see how patterns compared to other patterns in history. I think eventually I can get to intraday patterns but right now it’s just a full day that you are search so the prime use case for penny stocks imo would be to see the 2nd day after a crazy day, see if it’s likely to continue or reverse. Maybe you can find some other use cases. But we should have all the tickers. Let me know if you try a ticker and we don’t have it!

comments.author: Diligent-Wear7458
comments.body: Really cool dude. How are you handling payments? You're primed for x402 on the agent end.

comments.author: atlasayn
comments.body:
Really interesting approach — pattern matching against 24M historical embeddings is a solid signal source. The regime-filtered win rates are the right idea too.

One thing this pairs well with: a pre-trade policy layer that takes the pattern signal and enforces risk limits before execution. The agent gets "historically this setup returned X with Y win rate" from your tool, but still needs something to say "ok but you're already at 60% exposure so max additional size is 5%" or "regime is high-volatility so block new longs entirely."

Pattern recognition tells you what to trade. Risk governance decides how much and whether you're allowed to. Two separate concerns that work best as separate layers. Been building exactly the governance side as an MCP server — would be a natural complement to Chart Library in a multi-tool agent stack.

comments.author: Far-Tone886
comments.body:
This is actually really interesting. The idea of letting an agent query “what happened the last time this setup appeared” is way more useful than static backtests.

One thing I’ve noticed though when working with AI agents is that more data doesn’t always translate into better decisions. Sometimes it just creates more noise unless there’s a clear framework for acting on it.

I’ve been exploring more structured AI trading for this, especially with multi pair setups. You can set strategies and risk rules, and it handles execution while showing the reasoning behind each trade. You can also add rules like pausing after drawdowns or loss streaks.

What made a difference for me was not just having more inputs, but seeing how the system actually behaves under constraints and over time.

Curious how you’re thinking about that part. Is the agent just surfacing patterns, or actually using them to make and manage trades?
